findings.suggestion: Use the comma-ok idiom and guard nil: `u, ok := users[id]; if !ok || u == nil { return "", false }` (change signature to `(string, bool)`), or return "" if the signature must remain `string`. Then add a test covering the missing-id path (e.g. `FindUser(map[int]*User{}, 1)` must not panic) and run `go test ./...` / `go vet ./...` to close the verification gate.
findings.summary: source.go:13-14: `u := users[id]; return u.Email` indexes the map without the comma-ok check. A missing id yields a nil *User (map zero value), and a present-but-nil pointer does too; dereferencing u.Email then panics and crashes the process. The agent's own BUG comment (lines 10-12) documents this but the code was committed unfixed — a known crash left in place.
findings.title: Nil-pointer dereference in FindUser on missing/nil map entry

findings.suggestion: Use the comma-ok form and handle the miss without dereferencing: `u, ok := users[id]; if !ok || u == nil { return "" }` (or better, change the signature to `(string, bool)` or `(string, error)` so callers can distinguish 'no such user' from 'user with empty email').
findings.summary: source.go:13-14: `u := users[id]` returns the zero value `nil` for any `id` not present in the `map[int]*User`, then `return u.Email` dereferences that nil pointer, causing a runtime panic ('invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference') that crashes the process. No comma-ok guard (`u, ok := users[id]`) exists. Same crash occurs if a key maps to an explicit nil `*User`. FindUser is exported, so any caller performing a not-found lookup crashes instead of getting a safe 'not found' result.
findings.title: FindUser panics on missing/nil map entry (nil-pointer dereference)

findings.suggestion: Use the comma-ok form and guard for nil before dereferencing: `u, ok := users[id]; if !ok || u == nil { return "" }; return u.Email` (or return an error to signal not-found).
findings.summary: FindUser (source.go:9) relies on the unenforced invariant that `id` exists in `users`. Line 13 `u := users[id]` uses a non-comma-ok map read, so an absent id binds `u = nil` (zero value of `*User`), and line 14 `return u.Email` dereferences that nil pointer, panicking and crashing the process. The comment at lines 10–12 documents this exact defect; nothing in the function guards against it.
findings.title: Nil-pointer dereference on map miss in FindUser

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Single newly-added file with a confirmed nil-pointer dereference. The function assumes every looked-up id is present and maps to a non-nil pointer; Go map semantics return nil for a missing key, so `u.Email` panics on any miss. Assumption is relied upon but not enforced. Currently 0 callers, so no live crash yet, but the contract is broken for any future caller.

The single new function FindUser has a guaranteed nil-pointer dereference: a map lookup miss (or a nil pointer value) at source.go:13 yields nil, and source.go:14 dereferences it without a guard, panicking and crashing the process. This is a real, language-guaranteed correctness/product-safety defect (a not-found lookup should degrade gracefully, not crash). Exported symbol with 0 current callers, so no live regression yet, but it is unsafe for any future caller. Fix with the comma-ok pattern and a nil check, ideally returning a found/error flag.

Single new file with a self-documented, unfixed nil-pointer dereference. FindUser dereferences the result of a map lookup without a comma-ok / nil check, so any absent id (or a present nil pointer) panics and crashes the process. Currently 0 callers, which bounds live impact (hence high, not critical), but the contract is broken for the first real caller. No tests exist and no fresh verification was run — the rerun_relevant_verification gate remains blocked until a missing-id test is added and `go test`/`go vet` is executed.
